ACCT 210: Introduction to managerial accounting, focusing on concepts, relationships, and procedures involved in the strategic decision-making process of managers in for-profit business organizations. Covers cost behavior, cost-volume-profit analysis, budgeting, standard costs, variance analysis, and the use of accounting data in planning and control.
ACCT 250: Introduction to financial accounting, including the conceptual framework underlying financial reporting, recording and reporting of business transactions, and preparation and analysis of financial statements (balance sheet, income statement, statement of cash flows). Emphasizes how external users interpret accounting information.
ACCT 310: Intermediate financial accounting with an in-depth study of the conceptual framework, financial statement preparation, revenue recognition, and accounting for current and long-term assets. Develops the ability to apply Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) to complex transactions.
ACCT 400a: Advanced financial accounting topics including consolidations, business combinations, foreign currency transactions, partnerships, and accounting for governmental and not-for-profit entities. Builds on intermediate accounting principles to address complex reporting situations.
ACCT 420: Principles of federal taxation, with an emphasis on how individuals are taxed. Integrates tax compliance, tax planning, financial accounting, economics, and finance. Students apply the tax framework to a variety of taxation scenarios involving income, deductions, credits, and property transactions.
ACCT 430: Examines the role of the external auditor and the audit process. Topics include professional standards, audit risk, internal controls, evidence gathering, sampling, and audit reports. Develops critical thinking around assurance services and the public accounting profession.
BNAN 276: First course in business statistics, with an emphasis on techniques for data analysis and inference in management. Covers descriptive statistics, probability, sampling distributions, estimation, hypothesis testing, and regression. Technical material is supplemented with applications drawn from real-world business situations.
BNAN 277: Introduces analytical methods for turning business data into insight. Covers data preparation, exploratory analysis, predictive modeling, and communicating results. Students work with real datasets using modern tools to support decision making in marketing, operations, and finance.
FIN 360: Develops the theory and practice of financial decision making. Covers time value of money, valuation of stocks and bonds, capital budgeting, capital structure, cost of capital, and the relationship between risk and return. Foundational course for the finance major.
MIS 111: Introduces students to concepts of computer technology and the impact of the Internet on social, organizational, personal, and ethical issues. Provides hands-on experience with productivity software (spreadsheets, databases) and develops a critical understanding of information systems.
MKTG 355: Survey of contemporary marketing concepts and practices. Examines the role of marketing in the firm and society, the marketing mix (product, price, place, promotion), consumer behavior, market segmentation, and the development of marketing strategy.
MKTG 361: Examines the psychological, sociological, and cultural factors that influence consumer decision making. Topics include perception, learning, motivation, attitudes, group influence, and the consumer decision process. Applies consumer behavior theory to marketing strategy.
MKTG 376: Principles and practice of professional selling and sales management. Covers the sales process, prospecting, presentations, handling objections, closing, account management, and the recruitment, training, and motivation of sales personnel.
OSCM 471: Introduction to operations and supply chain management. Covers process analysis, capacity planning, inventory management, quality management, lean operations, and supply chain design. Emphasizes how operations decisions create value for customers and the firm.
BCOM 212: Develops written and oral business communication skills. Students practice writing professional emails, memos, reports, and proposals, and deliver effective business presentations. Emphasizes audience analysis, clarity, persuasion, and ethical communication.
BNAD 301: Foundational course on the theory and practice of management. Covers planning, organizing, leading, and controlling; organizational behavior; leadership; teams; motivation; and the management of change. Prepares students to work effectively in modern organizations.
BNAD 302: Examines management practice across cultural, economic, and political contexts. Topics include cultural intelligence, ethics, sustainability, global strategy, and the social responsibilities of business. Develops the perspective and skills needed to manage in a global environment.
MGMT 410: Strategic management and policy. Integrates knowledge from across the business disciplines to analyze how firms create and sustain competitive advantage. Uses the case method to examine industry analysis, business and corporate strategy, and strategy implementation.
MGMT 420: Survey of the legal environment of business. Covers contracts, torts, agency, business organizations, employment law, intellectual property, and government regulation. Develops the ability to recognize legal issues in business decisions and to work effectively with legal counsel.
